mnemonics.title: VIRTUAL
mnemonics.content: Virtual In Runtime, Actual Late-bound Invocation — Remember: virtual functions are resolved at runtime, not compile time, allowing the actual function to be called based on the object type.

mnemonics.title: VTABLE
mnemonics.content: Virtual TABLE — A lookup table created per class that stores pointers to virtual functions; used by vptr to resolve function calls at runtime.

mnemonics.title: P.A.I.R. for Override
mnemonics.content: P.A.I.R. = Polymorphism Achieved via Inheritance & Runtime binding — Base pointer can access derived class methods through virtual functions.

mnemonics.title: D.A.D. for Virtual Destructor
mnemonics.content: D.A.D. = Delete base class object ADvice — Always make base class destructor virtual to ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects when deleted through base pointer.

acronyms.usage: Hidden pointer added to each object of a class with virtual functions; points to the class's vtable. Used implicitly by compiler for runtime polymorphism.
Array of function pointers created per class; contains addresses of virtual functions. Accessed via vptr to achieve dynamic dispatch.
Mechanism that identifies object type at runtime; used by dynamic_cast and typeid operators to safely handle polymorphic objects.

acronyms.expansion: Pure Virtual Function / Pure Online Declaration
acronyms.usage: Pure virtual function (virtual void func() = 0;) makes class abstract; cannot instantiate, but derived classes must override.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a dispatch center (vtable) with a switchboard. Each object carries a remote (vptr) that connects to the right switch, which then connects to the correct function (TV channel). The remote decides which function runs.

Picture each object as a box with two sections: the vptr (first compartment) points to a separate chart (vtable) hanging on the wall, which lists all the function addresses. Derived class charts have modified entries.

Think of it like a funnel — a base class pointer (wide mouth) can hold any derived class object (narrower), but only 'sees' what the base defines. The actual object type determines which version runs.

Imagine a blank form (abstract class) with signature lines (pure virtual functions). You cannot submit the blank form — you must fill it completely (override all pure virtuals) to create a valid derived object.

analogies.concept: Base Class Pointer to Derived Object
analogies.analogy: Like a universal TV remote (base pointer) that can control any brand of TV (derived objects). The remote knows basic functions (base class interface), but the actual TV (derived object) determines how each button behaves.
Like a restaurant menu (base class) with 'Chef's Special' (virtual function). The actual dish served depends on which branch's kitchen (derived class) prepares it — decided when you order (runtime).
Like a postal system where the final delivery route isn't fixed at the post office (compile time) but determined by the address on the package (object type) at the moment of delivery (runtime).
Like having a master key (virtual destructor) that can open any locked room (derived class object). Without it, only the main door opens, leaving rooms inside unlocked and unsafe.
